quiz.title: Macroeconomics (Quiz 4)
quiz.subtitle: Unit 8 Quiz 4: Inflation-Control Targets, Operating Band, and Monetary Policy Transmission
quiz.subject: Economics
quiz.topic: Inflation Targeting, Bank of Canada Operating Band, Overnight Rate, Open Market Operations, Ample Reserves, Monetary Transmission Mechanism

questions.question: Which of the following issues do critics express about the use of an inflation-control target?
options: The policy control rests in the hands of civil servants rather than in the hands of elected officials.
The policy control rests in the hands of elected officials rather than in the hands of civil servants.
The target encourages a focus on real GDP growth at the expense of employment and of inflation.
The target encourages a focus on inflation at the expense of employment and real GDP growth.
Monetary policy tends to be sensitive to the state of employment while focusing on inflation control targets.
questions.explanation: Critics argue that a strict inflation-control target may cause the central bank to prioritize price stability over other macroeconomic goals, potentially sacrificing employment and real GDP growth when inflation threatens to exceed the target.

questions.question:
The Central Bank of Arcania has a strict inflation-control target of 2%. Suddenly, a global supply chain crisis causes the price of essential goods to skyrocket (Cost-Push Inflation), while simultaneously causing businesses to lay off workers due to high costs.

If the Central Bank of Arcania follows its inflation-control target strictly in this scenario, what is the most likely outcome?
options: They will lower interest rates to help businesses hire more workers, ignoring the inflation spike.
They will keep interest rates the same to maintain a neutral stance.
They will raise interest rates to combat inflation, potentially deepening the recession and increasing unemployment.
The inflation-control target will automatically shift to 5% to accommodate the crisis.
Tax rates will be lowered automatically to offset the interest rate changes.
questions.explanation: A strict inflation targeter must prioritize the price level. To bring inflation down from a supply shock, they would raise rates, which further reduces aggregate demand and can worsen unemployment.

questions.question:
The economy is currently experiencing an inflationary gap. The government is running a large budget deficit, and the Central Bank is committed to a strict inflation-control target.

If the Central Bank acts to meet its target while the government continues its deficit spending, what is the most likely result for the economy's interest rates and investment?
options: Interest rates will fall, leading to an increase in private investment.
Interest rates will rise, leading to a "crowding out" of private investment.
Interest rates will remain stable because the budget deficit offsets the inflation target.
The inflationary gap will automatically close without any change in interest rates.
The structural deficit will decrease as the Central Bank raises rates.
questions.explanation: The conflict: The government is "heating up" the economy with deficit spending (expansionary fiscal policy). The reaction: To hit its inflation target, the Central Bank must "cool down" the economy by raising interest rates (contractionary monetary policy). The result: Higher interest rates make borrowing more expensive for businesses, which reduces private investment spending. This is the classic "Crowding Out" effect.

questions.question:
The economy is currently facing stagflation (simultaneous high inflation and high unemployment). The Central Bank operates under a strict inflation-control target, while the Government is under pressure to reduce the unemployment rate.

If both the Central Bank and the Government aggressively pursue their primary mandates independently, what is the most likely outcome for the economy?
options: The inflationary gap and the recessionary gap will cancel each other out, leading to stability.
The Government's expansionary fiscal policy will likely undermine the Central Bank's efforts to hit its inflation target.
Both inflation and unemployment will fall simultaneously due to the combined policy effort.
The Central Bank will lower interest rates to assist the Government in job creation.
The structural deficit will disappear as the economy reaches equilibrium.
questions.explanation: In stagflation, the Central Bank raises rates to fight inflation (contractionary), while the Government increases spending or cuts taxes to fight unemployment (expansionary). These policies work at cross-purposes, and the fiscal stimulus will likely undermine the Central Bank's anti-inflation efforts.

questions.question: What defines the lower limit of the Bank of Canada's operating band?
options: the deposit rate
bank rate
the overnight rate
zero
the inflation rate
questions.explanation: The Bank of Canada's operating band for the overnight rate has a lower limit defined by the deposit rate—the interest rate the Bank pays on deposits held by financial institutions at the central bank.

questions.question: What defines the upper limit of the Bank of Canada's operating band?
options: bank rate
the deposit rate
the overnight rate
the overnight rate plus 1 percent
the upper limit of the inflation target range
questions.explanation: The upper limit of the Bank of Canada's operating band is the bank rate—the interest rate the Bank charges on loans to financial institutions. The overnight rate target sits at the midpoint of this band.

questions.question:
"Every five years, the Bank of Canada and the Government of Canada review and renew our agreement on Canada's monetary policy framework. In 2021, we renewed Canada's flexible inflation-targeting framework for 2022 to 2026."

Refer to the fact above. In the inflation control agreement, the Government of Canada and the Bank of Canada agree to all of the following statements except which one?
options: The target will continue to be defined in terms of the 12-month rate of change in the total CPI.
The inflation target will continue to be 2 percent per year.
The agreement will run for five years.
If the CPI becomes too volatile, in the future the target will be defined in terms of the 12-month rate of change in the core CPI.
The inflation-control range is 1 percent to 3 percent a year.
questions.explanation: The official inflation-control agreement defines the target in terms of total CPI, not core CPI. While the Bank monitors core CPI to understand underlying inflation trends, there is no provision to switch the official target to core CPI if total CPI becomes volatile.

questions.question:
The Bank of Canada notices that due to a sudden war abroad, oil prices have spiked, causing the Total CPI to rise to 4.5%. However, the Core CPI (which excludes gas) remains steady at 2.1%.

According to the current 2022–2026 inflation-control agreement, how should the Bank officially view this situation?
options: They should immediately change the official target to Core CPI.
They must ignore the Core CPI and recognize that the Total CPI has exceeded the 1% to 3% control range.
The agreement automatically expires if Total CPI hits 4.5% or greater.
They should average the Total CPI and Core CPI to find a new target of 3.3%.
They are only required to report on inflation once every five years when the agreement is renewed.
questions.explanation: The Bank is tethered to the Total CPI. Even if the volatility is "not their fault" (like a global oil spike), the official target remains the 12-month change in Total CPI. They use Core CPI to explain why it happened, but it doesn't change the goalpost.

questions.question: Select all that apply: In Canada, ample reserves ___________
options: help avoid bank failures
were created to increase economic growth
were an accidental creation during the Covid-19 recession
increase the cost of borrowing from the central bank
collapsed the need for reserve requirement
questions.explanation: Ample reserves help avoid bank failures by ensuring abundant liquidity in the banking system. They were an accidental creation during the Covid-19 recession when the Bank of Canada engaged in large-scale asset purchases. The system of ample reserves has collapsed the need for a traditional reserve requirement. They were not deliberately created to increase economic growth. Ample reserves do not increase the cost of borrowing from the central bank; the bank rate is set independently by monetary policy, not by the level of reserves.

questions.question: When the Bank of Canada conducts an open market purchase of government securities, which of the following events occurs?
options: Bank reserves decrease.
Bank loans increase.
The price of bonds decreases.
Inflation falls.
Credit conditions tighten.
questions.explanation: An open market purchase injects reserves into the banking system. With more reserves, banks can increase lending, so bank loans increase. Bond prices rise (not fall) due to increased demand, and credit conditions ease (not tighten).

questions.question: If the Bank of Canada uses the corridor system and aims to lower the overnight rate, what does it do?
options: It lowers the bank rate and the deposit rate, as well as buying government securities.
It lowers the bank rate and increases the deposit rate, as well as buying government securities.
It lowers the bank rate and the deposit rate, as well as selling government securities.
It raises the bank rate and the deposit rate, as well as buying government securities.
It raises the bank rate and the deposit rate, as well as selling government securities.
questions.explanation: To lower the overnight rate target, the Bank lowers both the bank rate (upper limit) and the deposit rate (lower limit), shifting the entire operating band downward. It also buys government securities to inject reserves and ensure the overnight rate stays near the new target.

questions.question: If the overnight rate is below target, which of the following actions does the Bank take? The Bank ________ securities to ________ bank reserves, which ________ the overnight rate.
options: buys; increase the quantity of; raises
sells; increase the supply of; lowers
buys; decrease the quantity of; raises
sells; decrease the quantity of; raises
buys; increase the supply of; lowers
questions.explanation: If the overnight rate is below target, there are too many reserves in the system. The Bank sells securities to drain reserves (decrease the quantity), which puts upward pressure on the overnight rate, raising it back to the target.

questions.question: How does the Bank of Canada change the overnight rate when faced with a forecast of recession?
options: The Bank lowers the overnight rate, sometimes close to zero.
The Bank lowers the overnight rate but very slowly and never close to zero.
The Bank raises the overnight rate temporarily.
The Bank raises the overnight rate very slightly.
The Bank holds the overnight rate steady and waits for more evidence of recession.
questions.explanation: To stimulate the economy during a recession forecast, the Bank lowers the overnight rate to encourage borrowing and spending. In severe recessions, it may lower rates close to zero (the effective lower bound).

questions.question: Which of the following statements best expresses the relationship between the 3-month Treasury bill rate and the overnight rate?
options: The rates are not similar because banks can not readily substitute between them.
The rates are similar because both are required to remain with the Bank's operating band.
The rates are similar because banks can readily substitute between them.
The rates are not similar because financial markets determine the Treasury bill rate whereas the Bank sets the overnight rate.
The rates are not similar because the Government of Canada sets the Treasury bill rate whereas the Bank sets the overnight rate.
questions.explanation: The 3-month Treasury bill rate and the overnight rate tend to move closely together because banks can readily substitute between lending overnight in the interbank market and holding short-term Treasury bills. Arbitrage keeps the rates similar.

questions.question: If the Bank of Canada lowers the overnight loans rate, how do the exchange rate and net exports change? The exchange rate ________, and net exports ________.
options: rises; increase
rises; decrease
falls; increase
falls; decrease
falls; do not change
questions.explanation: A lower overnight rate reduces Canadian interest rates relative to foreign rates. This makes Canadian assets less attractive, reducing demand for Canadian dollars. The exchange rate falls (depreciates), making exports cheaper and imports more expensive, so net exports increase.

questions.question: Which of the following events does not occur when the Bank of Canada lowers the overnight rate target?
options: The supply of loanable funds increases.
The long-term real interest rate falls.
Consumption expenditure increases.
The inflation rate decreases.
Investment increases.
questions.explanation: Lowering the overnight rate is expansionary monetary policy. It increases the supply of loanable funds, lowers real interest rates, and stimulates consumption and investment. This increases aggregate demand, which tends to raise the inflation rate, not decrease it.

questions.question: Shown is an economy with potential GDP of 320 billions. To return the economy to full employment, which of the following actions does the central bank take? The central bank ________ the overnight rate target and ________ government securities.
options: raises; sells
lowers; sells
lowers; buys
raises; buys
lowers; does not change its holdings of
questions.explanation: The economy is operating above potential GDP (an inflationary gap). To return to full employment, the central bank must use contractionary policy: raise the overnight rate target and sell government securities to reduce reserves and cool down aggregate demand.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered inflation-control targets, the Bank of Canada's operating band, and the monetary transmission mechanism.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! You understand the core monetary policy tools. Review the distinction between the bank rate and deposit rate, and how open market operations affect the overnight rate.
scoreMessages.50: A fair attempt. Focus on: 1) Lower limit = deposit rate. 2) Upper limit = bank rate. 3) Overnight rate below target → sell securities → drain reserves → raise rate. 4) Lower overnight rate → exchange rate falls → net exports rise.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Remember: Open market purchases inject reserves and increase bank loans. Lower rates stimulate AD and tend to increase inflation. Strict inflation targeting may worsen unemployment during supply shocks.
